question:
For each positive integer $n$, write the sum $\sum_{m=1}^n
1/m$ in the form $p_n/q_n$, where $p_n$ and $q_n$ are relatively prime
positive integers.  Determine all $n$ such that 5 does not divide $q_n$.
solution:
The only such $n$ are the numbers 1--4, 20--24, 100--104, and
120--124.  For the proof let
\[H_n=\sum_{m=1}^n \frac{1}{m}\]
and introduce the auxiliary function
\[I_n=\sum_{1\leq m\leq n, (m,5)=1} \frac{1}{m}.\]
It is immediate (e.g., by induction) that
$I_n\equiv 1,-1,1,0,0$ (mod $5$) for $n\equiv  1,2,3,4,5$ (mod 5)
respectively, and moreover, we have the equality
\[\label{(*)}
H_n= \sum_{m=0}^k \frac{1}{5^m} I_{\lfloor n/5^m \rfloor},\]
where $k=k(n)$ denotes the largest integer such that $5^k\leq n$.
We wish to determine those $n$ such that the above sum has nonnegative
5--valuation.  (By the 5--valuation of a number $a$ we mean
the largest integer $v$ such that $a/5^v$ is an integer.)

If $\lfloor n/5^k \rfloor\leq 3$, then the last term in the above sum
has 5--valuation $-k$, since $I_1$, $I_2$, $I_3$ each have valuation
0; on the other hand, all other terms must have 5--valuation strictly
larger than $-k$.  It follows that $H_n$ has 5--valuation exactly
$-k$; in particular, $H_n$ has nonnegative 5--valuation in this case
if and only if $k=0$, i.e., $n=1$, 2, or 3.

Suppose now that $\lfloor n/5^k \rfloor=4$.  Then we must also have
$20\leq \lfloor n/5^{k-1}\rfloor \leq 24$.  The former condition
implies that the last term of the above sum is $I_4/5^k=1/(12\cdot
5^{k-2})$, which has 5--valuation $-(k-2)$.

It is clear that $I_{20}\equiv I_{24}\equiv 0$ (mod 25); hence if $\lfloor
n/5^{k-1}\rfloor$ equals 20 or 24, then the second--to--last term of the
above sum (if it exists) has valuation at least $-(k-3)$.  The
third--to--last term (if it exists) is of the form $I_r/5^{k-2}$, so that
the sum of the last term and the third to last term takes the form
$(I_r+1/12)/5^{k-2}$.  Since $I_r$ can be congruent only to 0,1, or -1
(mod 5), and $1/12\equiv 3$ (mod 5), we conclude that the sum of the
last term and third--to--last term has valuation $-(k-2)$, while all other
terms have valuation strictly higher.  Hence $H_n$ has nonnegative
5--valuation in this case only when $k\leq 2$, leading to the values
$n=4$ (arising from $k=0$), 20,24 (arising from $k=1$ and $\lfloor
n/5^{k-1}\rfloor = 20$ and 24 resp.), 101, 102, 103, and 104 (arising
from $k=2$, $\lfloor n/5^{k-1}\rfloor = 20$) and 120, 121, 122, 123,
and 124 (arising from $k=2$, $\lfloor n/5^{k-1}\rfloor=24$).

Finally, suppose $\lfloor n/5^k \rfloor=4$ and $\lfloor n/5^{k-1}
\rfloor=21$, 22, or 23.  Then as before, the first condition
implies that the last term of the sum in (*) has valuation $-(k-2)$,
while the second condition implies that the second--to--last term in the
same sum has valuation $-(k-1)$.  Hence all terms in the sum (*) have
5--valuation strictly higher than $-(k-1)$, except for the
second--to--last term, and therefore $H_n$ has 5--valuation $-(k-1)$ in
this case.  In particular, $H_n$ is integral (mod 5) in this case if and
only if  $k\leq 1$, which gives the additional values $n=21$, 22, and 23.

descriptive_long.question: For each positive integer $indexpositive$, write the sum $\sum_{summationindex=1}^{indexpositive} 1/summationindex$ in the form $numeratorfraction/denominatorfraction$, where $numeratorfraction$ and $denominatorfraction$ are relatively prime positive integers.  Determine all $indexpositive$ such that 5 does not divide $denominatorfraction$.
descriptive_long.solution:
The only such $indexpositive$ are the numbers 1--4, 20--24, 100--104, and
120--124.  For the proof let
\[harmonicpartial=\sum_{summationindex=1}^{indexpositive} \frac{1}{summationindex}\]
and introduce the auxiliary function
\[coprimeharmonic=\sum_{1\leq summationindex\leq indexpositive, (summationindex,5)=1} \frac{1}{summationindex}.\]
It is immediate (e.g., by induction) that
$coprimeharmonic\equiv 1,-1,1,0,0$ (mod $5$) for $indexpositive\equiv  1,2,3,4,5$ (mod 5)
respectively, and moreover, we have the equality
\[\label{(*)}
harmonicpartial= \sum_{summationindex=0}^{powerindex} \frac{1}{5^{summationindex}} I_{\lfloor indexpositive/5^{summationindex} \rfloor},\]
where $powerindex=powerindex(indexpositive)$ denotes the largest integer such that $5^{powerindex}\leq indexpositive$.
We wish to determine those $indexpositive$ such that the above sum has nonnegative
5--valuation.  (By the 5--valuation of a number $generalnumber$ we mean
the largest integer $valuationpower$ such that $generalnumber/5^{valuationpower}$ is an integer.)

If $\lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex} \rfloor\leq 3$, then the last term in the above sum
has 5--valuation $-powerindex$, since $I_1$, $I_2$, $I_3$ each have valuation
0; on the other hand, all other terms must have 5--valuation strictly
larger than $-powerindex$.  It follows that $harmonicpartial$ has 5--valuation exactly
$-powerindex$; in particular, $harmonicpartial$ has nonnegative 5--valuation in this case
if and only if $powerindex=0$, i.e., $indexpositive=1$, 2, or 3.

Suppose now that $\lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex} \rfloor=4$.  Then we must also have
$20\leq \lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex-1}\rfloor \leq 24$.  The former condition
implies that the last term of the above sum is $I_4/5^{powerindex}=1/(12\cdot
5^{powerindex-2})$, which has 5--valuation $-(powerindex-2)$.

It is clear that $I_{20}\equiv I_{24}\equiv 0$ (mod 25); hence if $\lfloor
indexpositive/5^{powerindex-1}\rfloor$ equals 20 or 24, then the second--to--last term of the
above sum (if it exists) has valuation at least $-(powerindex-3)$.  The
third--to--last term (if it exists) is of the form $I_{residualindex}/5^{powerindex-2}$, so that
the sum of the last term and the third to last term takes the form
$(I_{residualindex}+1/12)/5^{powerindex-2}$.  Since $I_{residualindex}$ can be congruent only to 0,1, or -1
(mod 5), and $1/12\equiv 3$ (mod 5), we conclude that the sum of the
last term and third--to--last term has valuation $-(powerindex-2)$, while all other
terms have valuation strictly higher.  Hence $harmonicpartial$ has nonnegative
5--valuation in this case only when $powerindex\leq 2$, leading to the values
$indexpositive=4$ (arising from $powerindex=0$), 20,24 (arising from $powerindex=1$ and $\lfloor
indexpositive/5^{powerindex-1}\rfloor = 20$ and 24 resp.), 101, 102, 103, and 104 (arising
from $powerindex=2$, $\lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex-1}\rfloor = 20$) and 120, 121, 122, 123,
and 124 (arising from $powerindex=2$, $\lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex-1}\rfloor=24$).

Finally, suppose $\lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex} \rfloor=4$ and $\lfloor indexpositive/5^{powerindex-1}
\rfloor=21$, 22, or 23.  Then as before, the first condition
implies that the last term of the sum in (*) has valuation $-(powerindex-2)$,
while the second condition implies that the second--to--last term in the
same sum has valuation $-(powerindex-1)$.  Hence all terms in the sum (*) have
5--valuation strictly higher than $-(powerindex-1)$, except for the
second--to--last term, and therefore $harmonicpartial$ has 5--valuation $-(powerindex-1)$ in
this case.  In particular, $harmonicpartial$ is integral (mod 5) in this case if and
only if  $powerindex\leq 1$, which gives the additional values $indexpositive=21$, 22, and 23.

descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
The only such $sunflower$ are the numbers 1--4, 20--24, 100--104, and
120--124.  For the proof let
\[cloudberry=\sum_{sailboat=1}^{sunflower} \frac{1}{sailboat}\]
and introduce the auxiliary function
\[rattlesnake=\sum_{1\leq sailboat\leq sunflower, (sailboat,5)=1} \frac{1}{sailboat}.\]
It is immediate (e.g., by induction) that
$rattlesnake\equiv 1,-1,1,0,0$ (mod $5$) for $sunflower\equiv  1,2,3,4,5$ (mod 5)
respectively, and moreover, we have the equality
\[\label{(*)}
cloudberry= \sum_{sailboat=0}^{marshmallow} \frac{1}{5^{sailboat}} rattlesnake_{\lfloor sunflower/5^{sailboat} \rfloor},\]
where $marshmallow=marshmallow(sunflower)$ denotes the largest integer such that $5^{marshmallow}\leq sunflower$.
We wish to determine those $sunflower$ such that the above sum has nonnegative
5--valuation.  (By the 5--valuation of a number $watermelon$ we mean
the largest integer $chocolate$ such that $watermelon/5^{chocolate}$ is an integer.)

If $\lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow} \rfloor\leq 3$, then the last term in the above sum
has 5--valuation $-marshmallow$, since $rattlesnake_1$, $rattlesnake_2$, $rattlesnake_3$ each have valuation
0; on the other hand, all other terms must have 5--valuation strictly
larger than $-marshmallow$.  It follows that $cloudberry$ has 5--valuation exactly
$-marshmallow$; in particular, $cloudberry$ has nonnegative 5--valuation in this case
if and only if $marshmallow=0$, i.e., $sunflower=1$, 2, or 3.

Suppose now that $\lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow} \rfloor=4$.  Then we must also have
$20\leq \lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow-1}\rfloor \leq 24$.  The former condition
implies that the last term of the above sum is $rattlesnake_4/5^{marshmallow}=1/(12\cdot
5^{marshmallow-2})$, which has 5--valuation $-(marshmallow-2)$.

It is clear that $rattlesnake_{20}\equiv rattlesnake_{24}\equiv 0$ (mod 25); hence if $\lfloor
sunflower/5^{marshmallow-1}\rfloor$ equals 20 or 24, then the second--to--last term of the
above sum (if it exists) has valuation at least $-(marshmallow-3)$.  The
third--to--last term (if it exists) is of the form $rattlesnake_{cheesecake}/5^{marshmallow-2}$, so that
the sum of the last term and the third to last term takes the form
$(rattlesnake_{cheesecake}+1/12)/5^{marshmallow-2}$.  Since $rattlesnake_{cheesecake}$ can be congruent only to 0,1, or -1
(mod 5), and $1/12\equiv 3$ (mod 5), we conclude that the sum of the
last term and third--to--last term has valuation $-(marshmallow-2)$, while all other
terms have valuation strictly higher.  Hence $cloudberry$ has nonnegative
5--valuation in this case only when $marshmallow\leq 2$, leading to the values
$sunflower=4$ (arising from $marshmallow=0$), 20,24 (arising from $marshmallow=1$ and $\lfloor
sunflower/5^{marshmallow-1}\rfloor = 20$ and 24 resp.), 101, 102, 103, and 104 (arising
from $marshmallow=2$, $\lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow-1}\rfloor = 20$) and 120, 121, 122, 123,
and 124 (arising from $marshmallow=2$, $\lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow-1}\rfloor=24$).

Finally, suppose $\lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow} \rfloor=4$ and $\lfloor sunflower/5^{marshmallow-1}
\rfloor=21$, 22, or 23.  Then as before, the first condition
implies that the last term of the sum in (*) has valuation $-(marshmallow-2)$,
while the second condition implies that the second--to--last term in the
same sum has valuation $-(marshmallow-1)$.  Hence all terms in the sum (*) have
5--valuation strictly higher than $-(marshmallow-1)$, except for the
second--to--last term, and therefore $cloudberry$ has 5--valuation $-(marshmallow-1)$ in
this case.  In particular, $cloudberry$ is integral (mod 5) in this case if and
only if  $marshmallow\leq 1$, which gives the additional values $sunflower=21$, 22, and 23.

descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
The only such $boundless$ are the numbers 1--4, 20--24, 100--104, and
120--124.  For the proof let
\[disharmonicsum_{boundless}=\sum_{aggregate=1}^{boundless} \frac{1}{aggregate}\]
and introduce the auxiliary function
\[principalfunc_{boundless}=\sum_{1\leq aggregate\leq boundless, (aggregate,5)=1} \frac{1}{aggregate}.\]
It is immediate (e.g., by induction) that
$principalfunc_{boundless}\equiv 1,-1,1,0,0$ (mod $5$) for $boundless\equiv  1,2,3,4,5$ (mod 5)
respectively, and moreover, we have the equality
\[\label{(*)}
disharmonicsum_{boundless}= \sum_{aggregate=0}^{minimalorder} \frac{1}{5^{aggregate}} principalfunc_{\lfloor boundless/5^{aggregate} \rfloor},\]
where $minimalorder=minimalorder(boundless)$ denotes the largest integer such that $5^{minimalorder}\leq boundless$.
We wish to determine those $boundless$ such that the above sum has nonnegative
5--valuation.  (By the 5--valuation of a number $voidentity$ we mean
the largest integer $devaluation$ such that $voidentity/5^{devaluation}$ is an integer.)

If $\lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder} \rfloor\leq 3$, then the last term in the above sum
has 5--valuation $-minimalorder$, since $principalfunc_1$, $principalfunc_2$, $principalfunc_3$ each have valuation
0; on the other hand, all other terms must have 5--valuation strictly
larger than $-minimalorder$.  It follows that $disharmonicsum_{boundless}$ has 5--valuation exactly
$-minimalorder$; in particular, $disharmonicsum_{boundless}$ has nonnegative 5--valuation in this case
if and only if $minimalorder=0$, i.e., $boundless=1$, 2, or 3.

Suppose now that $\lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder} \rfloor=4$.  Then we must also have
$20\leq \lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder-1}\rfloor \leq 24$.  The former condition
implies that the last term of the above sum is $principalfunc_4/5^{minimalorder}=1/(12\cdot
5^{minimalorder-2})$, which has 5--valuation $-(minimalorder-2)$.

It is clear that $principalfunc_{20}\equiv principalfunc_{24}\equiv 0$ (mod 25); hence if $\lfloor
boundless/5^{minimalorder-1}\rfloor$ equals 20 or 24, then the second--to--last term of the
above sum (if it exists) has valuation at least $-(minimalorder-3)$.  The
third--to--last term (if it exists) is of the form $principalfunc_{irrelevant}/5^{minimalorder-2}$, so that
the sum of the last term and the third to last term takes the form
$(principalfunc_{irrelevant}+1/12)/5^{minimalorder-2}$.  Since $principalfunc_{irrelevant}$ can be congruent only to 0,1, or -1
(mod 5), and $1/12\equiv 3$ (mod 5), we conclude that the sum of the
last term and third--to--last term has valuation $-(minimalorder-2)$, while all other
terms have valuation strictly higher.  Hence $disharmonicsum_{boundless}$ has nonnegative
5--valuation in this case only when $minimalorder\leq 2$, leading to the values
$boundless=4$ (arising from $minimalorder=0$), 20,24 (arising from $minimalorder=1$ and $\lfloor
boundless/5^{minimalorder-1}\rfloor = 20$ and 24 resp.), 101, 102, 103, and 104 (arising
from $minimalorder=2$, $\lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder-1}\rfloor = 20$) and 120, 121, 122, 123,
and 124 (arising from $minimalorder=2$, $\lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder-1}\rfloor=24$).

Finally, suppose $\lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder} \rfloor=4$ and $\lfloor boundless/5^{minimalorder-1}
\rfloor=21$, 22, or 23.  Then as before, the first condition
implies that the last term of the sum in (*) has valuation $-(minimalorder-2)$,
while the second condition implies that the second--to--last term in the
same sum has valuation $-(minimalorder-1)$.  Hence all terms in the sum (*) have
5--valuation strictly higher than $-(minimalorder-1)$, except for the
second--to--last term, and therefore $disharmonicsum_{boundless}$ has 5--valuation $-(minimalorder-1)$ in
this case.  In particular, $disharmonicsum_{boundless}$ is integral (mod 5) in this case if and
only if  $minimalorder\leq 1$, which gives the additional values $boundless=21$, 22, and 23.

garbled_string.solution:
The only such $qwplrznf$ are the numbers 1--4, 20--24, 100--104, and
120--124.  For the proof let
\[xbrnegal=\sum_{xidfuhas=1}^{qwplrznf} \frac{1}{xidfuhas}\]
and introduce the auxiliary function
\[jvcoqmet=\sum_{1\leq xidfuhas\leq qwplrznf, (xidfuhas,5)=1} \frac{1}{xidfuhas}.\]
It is immediate (e.g., by induction) that
$jvcoqmet\equiv 1,-1,1,0,0$ (mod $5$) for $qwplrznf\equiv  1,2,3,4,5$ (mod 5)
respectively, and moreover, we have the equality
\[\label{(*)}
xbrnegal= \sum_{xidfuhas=0}^{lztnedqw} \frac{1}{5^{xidfuhas}} jvcoqmet_{\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{xidfuhas} \rfloor},\]
where $lztnedqw=lztnedqw(qwplrznf)$ denotes the largest integer such that $5^{lztnedqw}\leq qwplrznf$.
We wish to determine those $qwplrznf$ such that the above sum has nonnegative
5--valuation.  (By the 5--valuation of a number $zkruhcpe$ we mean
the largest integer $ypqmsrli$ such that $zkruhcpe/5^{ypqmsrli}$ is an integer.)

If $\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw} \rfloor\leq 3$, then the last term in the above sum
has 5--valuation $-lztnedqw$, since $jvcoqmet_1$, $jvcoqmet_2$, $jvcoqmet_3$ each have valuation
0; on the other hand, all other terms must have 5--valuation strictly
larger than $-lztnedqw$.  It follows that $xbrnegal$ has 5--valuation exactly
$-lztnedqw$; in particular, $xbrnegal$ has nonnegative 5--valuation in this case
if and only if $lztnedqw=0$, i.e., $qwplrznf=1$, 2, or 3.

Suppose now that $\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw} \rfloor=4$.  Then we must also have
$20\leq \lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw-1}\rfloor \leq 24$.  The former condition
implies that the last term of the above sum is $jvcoqmet_4/5^{lztnedqw}=1/(12\cdot
5^{lztnedqw-2})$, which has 5--valuation $-(lztnedqw-2)$.

It is clear that $jvcoqmet_{20}\equiv jvcoqmet_{24}\equiv 0$ (mod 25); hence if $\lfloor
qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw-1}\rfloor$ equals 20 or 24, then the second--to--last term of the
above sum (if it exists) has valuation at least $-(lztnedqw-3)$.  The
third--to--last term (if it exists) is of the form $jvcoqmet_{sfnlgxod}/5^{lztnedqw-2}$, so that
the sum of the last term and the third to last term takes the form
$(jvcoqmet_{sfnlgxod}+1/12)/5^{lztnedqw-2}$.  Since $jvcoqmet_{sfnlgxod}$ can be congruent only to 0,1, or -1
(mod 5), and $1/12\equiv 3$ (mod 5), we conclude that the sum of the
last term and third--to--last term has valuation $-(lztnedqw-2)$, while all other
terms have valuation strictly higher.  Hence $xbrnegal$ has nonnegative
5--valuation in this case only when $lztnedqw\leq 2$, leading to the values
$qwplrznf=4$ (arising from $lztnedqw=0$), 20,24 (arising from $lztnedqw=1$ and $\lfloor
qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw-1}\rfloor = 20$ and 24 resp.), 101, 102, 103, and 104 (arising
from $lztnedqw=2$, $\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw-1}\rfloor = 20$) and 120, 121, 122, 123,
and 124 (arising from $lztnedqw=2$, $\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw-1}\rfloor=24$).

Finally, suppose $\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw} \rfloor=4$ and $\lfloor qwplrznf/5^{lztnedqw-1}
\rfloor=21$, 22, or 23.  Then as before, the first condition
implies that the last term of the sum in (*) has valuation $-(lztnedqw-2)$,
while the second condition implies that the second--to--last term in the
same sum has valuation $-(lztnedqw-1)$.  Hence all terms in the sum (*) have
5--valuation strictly higher than $-(lztnedqw-1)$, except for the
second--to--last term, and therefore $xbrnegal$ has 5--valuation $-(lztnedqw-1)$ in
this case.  In particular, $xbrnegal$ is integral (mod 5) in this case if and
only if  $lztnedqw\leq 1$, which gives the additional values $qwplrznf=21$, 22, and 23.

kernel_variant.question:
For every positive integer $n$ write the harmonic sum
\[H_n=\sum_{m=1}^{n}\frac1m\]
in lowest terms as $H_n=\dfrac{p_n}{q_n}$ with $\gcd(p_n,q_n)=1$.  Determine all $n$ for which $7\nmid q_n$ (i.e. for which the reduced denominator is not divisible by $7$).
kernel_variant.solution:
Let H_n=\sum _{m=1}^n1/m=p_n/q_n in lowest terms and define
  I_m=\sum _{1\leq k\leq m,(k,7)=1}1/k.
A standard block-sum argument shows
  H_n=\sum _{j=0}^k I_{\lfloor n/7^j\rfloor }/7^j,
where k is the largest integer with 7^k\leq n.  Hence
  v_7(H_n)=min_{0\leq j\leq k}(v_7(I_{\lfloor n/7^j\rfloor })-j).

Step 1.  Compute I_r for 0\leq r\leq 6.  The inverses of 1,\ldots ,6 mod 7 are 1,4,5,2,3,6, so
  I_0=0 (v_7=\infty ),
  I_r\equiv 0 mod 7 \Leftrightarrow  r=6,
  I_r\equiv nonzero mod 7 for r=1,\ldots ,5.
Moreover H_6=49/20 so v_7(I_6)=2 and v_7(I_r)=0 for r=1,\ldots ,5.

Step 2.  Write n in base 7 as (a_k\ldots a_0)_7 with a_k\neq 0.  Then the j=k term has value I_{a_k}/7^k whose valuation is
  =0-k if 1\leq a_k\leq 5;
  =2-k if a_k=6.

- If a_k\in {1,\ldots ,5}, then for k>0 we get v_7(H_n)=-k<0, so the only solutions are k=0, a_0=1,\ldots ,6, i.e. n=1,\ldots ,6.

- If a_k=6 then 2-k must be \geq 0 \Rightarrow  k\leq 2.

Case k=1: n=(6a_0)_7=42+a_0, 0\leq a_0\leq 6 \Rightarrow  n=42,\ldots ,48.  Here v_7(H_n)=min(2-1, v_7(I_n)-0)\geq 0, so all n=42\ldots 48 work.

Case k=2: n=(6a_1a_0)_7=6\cdot 49 +7a_1 + a_0 =294+7a_1+a_0.  The j=2 term gives 2-2=0.  The j=1 term is I_{\lfloor n/7\rfloor }=I_{42+a_1} over 7, so its valuation is v_7(I_{42+a_1})-1.  We need v_7(I_{42+a_1})\geq 1.  Mod 7 one checks I_{42+a_1}\equiv I_{a_1}, and I_{a_1}\equiv 0 mod 7 iff a_1\in {0,6}.  Hence a_1=0 or 6.  Thus
  a_1=0 \Rightarrow  n=294,295,\ldots ,300;
  a_1=6 \Rightarrow  n=336,337,\ldots ,342.

No higher k is possible.  Therefore the full list of n with 7\nmid q_n is

  1,2,3,4,5,6;
  42,43,44,45,46,47,48;
  294,295,296,297,298,299,300;
  336,337,338,339,340,341,342.

core_steps: Introduce I_n (sum of reciprocals of numbers â‰¤ n that are coprime to 5) and record its residues mod 5.
Write H_n as the base-5 expansion  H_n = Î£_{m=0}^k I_{âŒŠn/5^mâŒ‹}/5^m.
Use 5-adic valuation to locate the smallest (i.e. most negative) power of 5 occurring among those summands.
Split into cases according to the least significant non-multiple-of-5 digit of n in base 5 (âŒŠn/5^kâŒ‹ = 1,2,3 or =4 with sub-cases for the next digit).
Identify all n for which the minimal valuation is â‰¥0, giving the final list.

slot1.description: Chosen prime that the denominator is tested against; whole argument works verbatim for any fixed prime p.

slot2.description: Residue pattern of I_n modulo the chosen prime (one value for each class mod p).

slot3.description: Set of base-p digits whose appearance as the last non-zero digit guarantees valuation exactly âˆ’k (here {1,2,3}).

slot4.description: Single critical digit that forces a second-level look-ahead (here digit 4 in base 5).

slot5.description: Range of two-digit base-p numbers for which the second-level term vanishes mod pÂ², causing the valuation test to pass.
slot5.original: 20â€“24 (base 5) i.e. numbers whose two least-significant base-5 digits are 4 0,â€¦,4 4

slot6.description: Final set of n that survive the valuation test; numerically changes with p but obtained via exactly the same case analysis.
